   474→2.1
   475→Latent conditional model
   476→We let context sentences and next tokens share the same latent space where the proba-
   477→bilistic inference happens. This lets us transform the next token prediction problem into
   478→the problem of learning various conditional probabilities among the latent variables. For a
   479→visualization of the framework, see Figure 1.
   480→Latent space of concepts
   481→We model the latent space with a set of binary random
   482→variables: VC = {C1,...,Cm}. Intuitively, one can view each latent binary random variable
   483→as representing the existence of a concept, e.g. positive vs negative sentiment, and VC
   484→contains all relevant concepts of interest for language modeling. Notation-wise, we will use
   485→C = (C1,...,Cm) to represent the latent random vector and c ∈{0,1}m to denote realizations
   486→of the latent random vector. For a index set I ⊆[m], CI (similarly, cI) denotes a subset of
   487→random variables. Note that the latent sample space C is the collection of binary vectors
   488→{0,1}m.
   489→These binary random variables are not necessarily independent. To model their depen-
   490→dencies, we assume that the variables form a Markov random field. That is, there is
   491→an undirected graph GC = (VC,EC) such that p(Ci|C[m]\i) = p(Ci|Cne(i)) for all i ∈[m]
   492→(where ne(i) denotes the set of neighbors of i), i.e. a variable is conditionally independent
   493→of all other variables given its neighbors. Markov random fields capture a wide variety
   494→of probability distributions, which include independent random variables as a special
   495→case.
   496→Remark 1. The theory readily handles directed acyclic graphical models (DAGs) [KF09],
   497→but we work with undirected graphs for notational simplicity.
   498→Latent space to next token (y)
   499→Let Y ∈Y be the random variable denoting the next
   500→token with sample space Y . We assume there exists an injective measurable function from
   501→concept space C to token space Y . That is, the value of C can always be read off from Y .
   502→This map induces a distribution on the next token (via the pushforward measure) that the
   503→LLM tries to learn. We denote the inverse of this map as hy (mapping tokens to concepts).
   504→The injectivity assumption is made for simplicity—with the injectivity assumption, for
   505→any given concept, one can identify pairs of tokens that only differ in that concept. This is
   506→useful for the theoretical analysis.
   507→Context sentence (x) to latent space
   508→Let X ∈X be the random variable denoting
   509→context sentence where X is the sample space, e.g. “The ruler of a kingdom is a” is an
   510→element of X . Intuitively, X contains partial information about the latent concepts that
   511→determine the next token Y . This is similar to a hidden Markov model where one can
   512→use previous observations to infer the current latent state. In other words, if one models
   513→language generation as transitions/drifts in the latent space [Aro+16], then given the
   514→context, one cannot fully determine but can make educated guesses on where the latent
   515→space might be transitioned into. Note that there’s inherent ambiguity involving the nex

   521→Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2025
   522→THE GEOMETRY OF CATEGORICAL AND HIERARCHI-
   523→CAL CONCEPTS IN LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
   524→Kiho Park, Yo Joong Choe, Yibo Jiang, and Victor Veitch
   525→University of Chicago
   526→ABSTRACT
   527→The linear representation hypothesis is the informal idea that semantic concepts
   528→are encoded as linear directions in the representation spaces of large language
   529→models (LLMs). Previous work has shown how to make this notion precise for
   530→representing binary concepts that have natural contrasts (e.g., {male, female})
   531→as directions in representation space. However, many natural concepts do not have
   532→natural contrasts (e.g., whether the output is about an animal). In this work, we
   533→show how to extend the formalization of the linear representation hypothesis to
   534→represent features (e.g., is animal) as vectors. This allows us to immediately
   535→formalize the representation of categorical concepts as polytopes in the represen-
   536→tation space. Further, we use the formalization to prove a relationship between
   537→the hierarchical structure of concepts and the geometry of their representations.
   538→We validate these theoretical results on the Gemma and LLaMA-3 large language
   539→models, estimating representations for 900+ hierarchically related concepts using
   540→data from WordNet.1
   541→1
   542→INTRODUCTION
   543→Understanding how high-level semantic meaning is encoded in the representation spaces of large
   544→language models (LLMs) is a fundamental problem in interpretability. A particularly promising
   545→avenue is the linear representation hypothesis (e.g., Mikolov et al., 2013; Elhage et al., 2022; Nanda
   546→et al., 2023; Gurnee & Tegmark, 2024; Park et al., 2024). This is the informal hypothesis that
   547→semantic concepts are represented linearly in the representation spaces of LLMs. To assess the
   548→validity of this hypothesis, and systematically build tools on top of it, we must make precise what it
   549→means for a concept to be linearly represented, and understand how semantics are encoded in (the
   550→geometry of) the representation spaces.
   551→Focusing on the final softmax layer, Park et al. (2024) give a formalization for the case of binary
   552→concepts that can be defined by counterfactual pairs of words. For example, the concept male ⇒
   553→female is formalized using the counterfactual pairs {(“man”, “woman”), (“king”, “queen”), . . . }.
   554→They prove that such binary concepts have a well-defined linear representation as a direction in the
   555→representation space. They further connect semantic structure and representation geometry by show-
   556→ing that, under a suitable inner product, causally separable concepts that can be freely manipulated
   557→(e.g., male ⇒female and french ⇒english) are represented by orthogonal directions.
   558→However, this formalization is limited: many natural concepts cannot be defined by counterfactual
   559→pairs of words. For example, simple binary features (e.g., is animal) or categorical concepts
   560→(e.g., {mammal, bird, reptile, fish}) do not admit such formalizations. Additionally, it is
   561→not clear how semantic r

   594→concepts (e.g., {mammal, bird, reptile, fish}) as polytopes where each vertex is the
   595→vector representation of one of the elements of the concept (e.g., is bird).
   596→2. Using this result, we show that semantic hierarchy between concepts is encoded geometri-
   597→cally as orthogonality between representations, in a manner we make precise.
   598→3. Finally, we empirically validate these theoretical results on the Gemma (Mesnard et al.,
   599→2024) and LLaMA-3 (Dubey et al., 2024) LLMs. To that end, we extract concepts from
   600→the WordNet hierarchy (Miller, 1995), estimate their representations, and show that the
   601→geometric structure of the representations aligns with the semantic hierarchy of WordNet.
   602→The final structure is remarkably simple, and is summarized in Figure 1.
   603→2
   604→PRELIMINARIES
   605→We begin with some necessary background.
   606→2.1
   607→LARGE LANGUAGE MODELS
   608→For the purposes of this paper, we consider a large language model to consist of two parts. The first
   609→part is a function λ that maps an input text x to a vector λ(x) in a representation space Λ ≃Rd.
   610→This is the function given by the stacked transformer blocks. We take λ(x) to be the output of the
   611→final layer at the final token position. The second part is an unembedding layer that assigns a vector
   612→γ(y) in an unembedding space Γ ≃Rd to each token y in the vocabulary. Together, these define a
   613→2
   614→
   615→--- Page 3 ---
   616→Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2025
   617→sampling distribution over tokens via the softmax distribution:
   618→P(y | x) =
   619→exp(λ(x)⊤γ(y))
   620→P
   621→y′∈Vocab exp(λ(x)⊤γ(y′)).
   622→(2.1)
   623→We note that the results that follow rely on the duality between the embedding and unembedding
   624→spaces, and the softmax link between them. Accordingly, we do not address the “internal” structure
   625→of the LLMs. However, we are optimistic that a clear understanding of the softmax geometry will
   626→shed light on this as well.
   627→2.2
   628→CONCEPTS
   629→We formalize a concept as a latent variable W that is caused by the context X and causes the output
   630→Y . That is, a concept is a thing that could—in principle—be manipulated to affect the output of the
   631→language model. In the particular case where a concept is a binary variable with a word-level coun-
   632→terfactual, we can identify the variable W with the counterfactual pair of outputs (Y (0), Y (1)).
   633→Concretely, we can identify male ⇒female with (Y (0), Y (1)) ∈R {(“man”, “woman”),
   634→(“king”, “queen”), (“he”, “her”), . . . }. We emphasize that the notion of a concept as a latent variable
   635→that affects the output is more general than the counterfactual binary case.
   636→Given a pair of concept variables W and Z, we say that W is causally separable with Z if the
   637→potential outcome Y (W = w, Z = z) is well-defined for all w, z. That is, two variables are
   638→causally separable if they can be freely manipulated—e.g., we can change the output language and
   639→the sex of the subject freely, so these concepts are causally separable.
   640→2.3
   641→CAUSAL INNER PRODUCT AND LINEAR REPRESENTATIONS
   642→We are trying to understand how concepts are represented. At this stage, there are two distinct
   643→representation spaces: Λ and Γ. The former is the space of context embeddings, and the latter is
   644→the space of token unembeddings. We would like to unify these spaces so that there is just a single
   645→notion of representation.
   646→Park et al. (2024) show how to achieve this unification via a “Causal Inner Product”. This is a par-
   647→ticular choice of inner product that respects the semantics of language in the sense that the linear
   648→representations of (binary, counterfactual) causally separable concepts are orthogonal under the in-
   649→ner product. Their result can be understood as saying that there is some invertible matrix A and
   650→constant vector ¯γ0 such that, if we transform the embedding and unembedding spaces as
   651→g(y) ←A(γ(y) −¯γ0),
   652→ℓ(x) ←A−⊤λ(x)
   653→(2.2)
   654→then the Euclidean inner product in the transformed spaces is the causal inner product, and the Riesz
   655→isomorphism between the embedding and unembedding spaces is simply the usual vector transpose
   656→operation. We can estimate A as the whitening operation for the unembedding matrix. Following
   657→this transformation, we can think of the embedding and unembedding spaces as the same space,
   658→equipped with the Euclidean inner product.2
   659→Notice that the softmax probabilities (eq. (2.1)) are unchanged for any A and ¯γ0, so this transfor-
   660→mation does not affect the model’s behavior. The vect

   666→AXBENCH: Steering LLMs? Even Simple Baselines
   667→Outperform Sparse Autoencoders
   668→Zhengxuan Wu * 1 Aryaman Arora * 1 Atticus Geiger 2 Zheng Wang 1 Jing Huang 1
   669→Dan Jurafsky 1 Christopher D. Manning 1 Christopher Potts 1
   670→Abstract
   671→Fine-grained steering of language model outputs
   672→is essential for safety and reliability. Prompting
   673→and finetuning are widely used to achieve these
   674→goals, but interpretability researchers have pro-
   675→posed a variety of representation-based techniques
   676→as well, including sparse autoencoders (SAEs),
   677→linear artificial tomography, supervised steering
   678→vectors, linear probes, and representation finetun-
   679→ing. At present, there is no benchmark for mak-
   680→ing direct comparisons between these proposals.
   681→Therefore, we introduce AXBENCH, a large-scale
   682→benchmark for steering and concept detection, and
   683→report experiments on Gemma-2-2B and 9B. For
   684→steering, we find that prompting outperforms all
   685→existing methods, followed by finetuning. For
   686→concept detection, representation-based methods
   687→such as difference-in-means, perform the best. On
   688→both evaluations, SAEs are not competitive. We
   689→introduce a novel weakly-supervised representa-
   690→tional method (Rank-1 Representation Finetun-
   691→ing; ReFT-r1), which is competitive on both tasks
   692→while providing the interpretability advantages
   693→that prompting lacks. Along with AXBENCH, we
   694→train and publicly release SAE-scale feature dic-
   695→tionaries for ReFT-r1 and DiffMean.
   696→https://github.com/stanfordnlp/axbench

   700→values. While prompting and finetuning are now widely

   754→AXBENCH
   755→data from an LLM. We evaluate model-control methods,
   756→including prompting and finetuning baselines, along two
   757→utility axes: concept detection C and model steering S .
   758→For the former, we use labelled synthetic data as ground
   759→truth; for the latter, we evaluate long-form generations using
   760→an LLM judge. The labelled training data enables com-
   761→parison between supervised dictionary-learning methods
   762→(SDLs) and unsupervised methods like SAEs. The bench-
   763→mark includes tasks generated from SAE concept lists for
   764→GemmaScope (Lieberum et al., 2024), covering two layers
   765→each from instruction-tuned Gemma-2-2B and Gemma-2-9B
   766→(Gemma Team et al., 2024). However, AXBENCH is by
   767→nature extensible to arbitrary concept descriptions: we in-
   768→tend to add new evaluation tasks as better feature-labelling
   769→techniques and new approaches to steering emerge.
   770→We evaluate a variety of steering methods—including a
   771→novel weakly-supervised method we introduce, ReFT-r1—
   772→along with prompting, full finetuning, and two parameter-
   773→efficient finetuning methods (LoRA and LoReFT). On steer-
   774→ing, only ReFT-r1 is competitive with finetuning and prompt-
   775→ing baselines, while SAEs fall behind both ReFT-r1 and
   776→difference-in-means (Marks and Tegmark, 2024) on both
   777→axes. While representation steering methods largely lag
   778→behind incumbent model-control techniques, ReFT-r1 is
   779→evidence that steering can be pushed further with the avail-
   780→ability of comprehensive evaluation benchmarks. Finally,
   781→along with AXBENCH, we train and publicly release SAE-
   782→scale feature dictionaries for ReFT-r1 and DiffMean.

   786→2. Related work
   787→Representation-based control.
   788→Interventional/causal in-
   789→terpretability has emerged as the dominant paradigm for
   790→understanding neural networks in the LLM era, enabling
   791→the reverse-engineering of circuits underlying specific be-
   792→haviours (Giulianelli et al., 2018; Vig et al., 2020; Geiger
   793→et al., 2021; 2022; Meng et al., 2022; Chan et al., 2022;
   794→Wang et al., 2023; Goldowsky-Dill et al., 2023; Geiger et al.,
   795→2024; Guerner et al., 2024; Geiger et al., 2024). An impor-
   796→tant assumption in much of this work is the linear represen-
   797→tation hypothesis, which claims that linear subspaces of rep-
   798→resentations in neural networks encode concepts (Mikolov
   799→et al., 2013b; Pennington et al., 2014; Bolukbasi et al., 2016;
   800→Elhage et al., 2022; Park et al., 2023; Nanda et al., 2023).

801→Intervening on representations has thus emerged as an alter-
   802→native to finetuning and prompting for LM control.

   887→Measuring Chain of Thought Faithfulness by Unlearning Reasoning Steps
   888→Martin Tutek1
   889→Fateme Hashemi Chaleshtori2
   890→Ana Marasovi´c2
   891→Yonatan Belinkov1
   892→1Technion - Israel Institute of Technology
   893→2University of Utah
   894→martin.tutek@gmail.com
   895→{fateme.hashemi, ana.marasovic}@utah.edu
   896→belinkov@technion.ac.il
   897→Abstract
   898→When prompted to think step-by-step, language
   899→models (LMs) produce a chain of thought
   900→(CoT), a sequence of reasoning steps that the
   901→model supposedly used to produce its predic-
   902→tion. Despite much work on CoT prompting
   903→it is unclear if reasoning verbalized in a CoT
   904→is faithful to the models’ parameteric beliefs.
   905→We introduce a framework for measuring para-
   906→metric faithfulness of generated reasoning, and
   907→propose Faithfulness by Unlearning Reason-
   908→ing steps (FUR), an instance of this framework.
   909→FUR erases information contained in reason-
   910→ing steps from model parameters, and mea-
   911→sures faithfulness as the resulting effect of the
   912→model’s prediction. Our experiments with four
   913→LMs and five multi-hop multi-choice question
   914→answering (MCQA) datasets show that FUR is
   915→frequently able to precisely change the underly-
   916→ing models’ prediction for a given instance by
   917→unlearning key steps, indicating when a CoT is
   918→parametrically faithful. Further analysis shows
   919→that CoTs generated by models post-unlearning
   920→support different answers, hinting at a deeper
   921→effect of unlearning.1
   922→1
   923→Introduction
   924→Language models (LMs) can perform various tasks
   925→accurately and verbalize some reasoning via a so-
   926→called chain of thought (CoT) (Kojima et al., 2022;
   927→Wei et al., 2022), even without specialized super-
   928→vised training. CoT reasoning is emerging as a pow-
   929→erful technique for improving the performance of
   930→LMs in complex tasks (OpenAI, 2024; DeepSeek-
   931→AI et al., 2025). It is not clear, however, whether
   932→the reasoning encoded in the CoT is a faithful rep-
   933→resentation of the internal reasoning process of the
   934→model, casting doubts about the reliability of CoT
   935→as a window onto the model’s ‘thought process’.
   936→Various works set out to explore CoT faithful-
   937→ness by perturbing tokens within the CoT and ob-

   966→We then segment the reasoning chain and unlearn con-

   971→serving whet
   972→--- Page 2 ---
   973→a framework in two stages: (1) an intervention on
   974→the model parameters, which aims to erase infor-
   975→mation in the CoT from model parameters; and (2)
   976→evaluating parametric faithfulness, i.e. whether the
   977→intervention affected the models’ prediction. See
   978→components in Figure 1. PFF is a general frame-
   979→work that can be instantiated with various inter-
   980→ventions and applied to different types of CoT and
   981→other free-text explanations.
   982→In this work, we propose an instance of PFF
   983→we call Faithfulness by Unlearning Reasoning
   984→steps (FUR), a machine unlearning-based (Cao
   985→and Yang, 2015) approach to assessing CoT faith-
   986→fulness. We use NPO (Zhang et al., 2024b), a
   987→preference-optimization-based unlearning method
   988→for PFF stage 1, the intervention on the model. We
   989→propose two metrics of quantifying faithfulness of
   990→reasoning steps: FF-HARD quantifies whether the
   991→CoT as a whole is faithful, while FF-SOFT identi-
   992→fies the most salient reasoning steps within the CoT.
   993→Concretely, we (a) generate a CoT, (b) segment it
   994→into steps, (c) independently unlearn knowledge
   995→encoded within each step from model parameters
   996→and (d) measure the effect of erased knowledge
   997→on the models’ prediction (Figure 1). If the target
   998→step was successfully and precisely unlearned, and
   999→the models’ prediction changed, the step faithfully
  1000→explains the models’ underlying reasoning process.
  1001→Through experimental evaluation on four LMs
  1002→and five MCQA multi-hop reasoning datasets, we
  1003→show we are able to perform valid interventions
  1004→that affect model predictions while retaining mod-
  1005→els’ general capabilities. In subsequent analyses
  1006→we show unlearning has a profound effect on the
  1007→model, modifying the answer supported by verbal-
  1008→ized reasoning post-unlearning. We also compare
  1009→parametric faithfulness to plausibility via a human
  1010→study, finding that humans do not consider steps
  1011→identified as important by FUR plausible. This find-
  1012→ing highlights a need for specialized alignment to
  1013→obtain CoTs that are both plausible and faithful.
  1014→The contributions of this work are as follows:
  1015→1. We introduce PFF, a framework for measuring
  1016→parametric faithfulness of LM reasoning.
  1017→2. We instantiate PFF with FUR using NPO, a
  1018→model unlearning method, and demonstrate
  1019→its effectiveness on unlearning fine-grained
  1020→reasoning steps.
  1021→3. We introduce FF-HARD and FF-SOFT, metrics
  1022→evaluating reasoning faithfulness, which can
  1023→be applied to full chains or individual steps.
  1024→4. We perform detailed analyses, including hu-
  1025→man and LLM-as-a-judge annotations, eval-
  1026→uating whether unlearning fundamentally
  1027→changes the verbalized reasoning, and if steps
  1028→identified as faithful are also plausible.
  1029→2
  1030→Background and Related Work
  1031→When CoT prompted, models exhibit better perfor-
  1032→mance on complex multi-hop and arithmetic rea-
  1033→soning tasks (Zhou et al., 2023; Fu et al., 2023b;
  1034→Sprague et al., 2025) compared to being prompted
  1035→directly (no-CoT). Chains of thought can be used as
  1036→additional context where models can store results
  1037→of intermediate hops, but they also 
  1038→--- Page 3 ---
  1039→soning could still be retrieved from the latent space
  1040→(Yang et al., 2024; Deng et al., 2024). To account
  1041→such confounders, we only use information from
  1042→generated CoTs to guide unlearning, while we gen-
  1043→erate predictions directly without CoTs, thus disen-
  1044→tangling contextual influence from the prediction.
  1045→Measuring Faithfulness.
  1046→Various tests and met-
  1047→rics for quantifying faithfulness of free-text ex-
  1048→planations in LMs have previously been pro-
  1049→posed (Lanham et al., 2023; Bentham et al.,
  1050→2024; Atanasova et al., 2023; Siegel et al., 2024).
  1051→By measuring properties such as sufficiency
  1052→through simulatability or counterfactual interven-
  1053→tions (Atanasova et al., 2023; Lanham et al., 2023),
  1054→these studies quantify susceptibility of the models’
  1055→predictions to changes in context or input. Such ap-
  1056→proaches are valid only if there is no direct causal
  1057→link between the input and prediction that bypasses
  1058→the explanation, which is rare in LMs (Bao et al.,
  1059→2025). In our work, we analyze whether paramet-
  1060→ric perturbations that affect the generated CoT also
  1061→affect the prediction. The closest to ours are the
  1062→contemporaneous works of Yeo et al. (2024) who
  1063→use activation patching to measure causal effect of
  1064→corrupting certain hidden states, and Zaman and
  1065→Srivastava (2025) who use knowledge editing to
  1066→evaluate existing (un)faithfulness metrics.
  1067→Background on Machine Unlearning.
  1068→Machine
  1069→unlearning aims to remove some and only some
  1070→undesired knowledge or behavior so as not to be
  1071→regurgitated by LMs (Cao and Yang, 2015; Harding
  1072→et al., 2019; Ippolito et al., 2023). There are multi-
  1073→ple approaches to unlearning for LMs, overviewed
  1074→in Geng et al. (2025) and Appendix A. They typi-
  1075→cally reduce the capability of the underlying LM
  1076→on target data, while retaining performance on re-
  1077→tain data and general capabilities. In this paper,
  1078→we unlearn reasoning steps by finetuning using the
  1079→negative preference optimization (NPO) loss on
  1080→the forget data (Zhang et al., 2024b) that discour-
  1081→ages the preference for forget sequences. We add
  1082→it to the KL divergence between the original and
  1083→“unlearned” model’s predictions on the retain set
  1084→(Chen and Yang, 2023; Yao et al., 2024). We chose
  1085→NPO+KL as it can be applied to unstructured text
  1086→and outperforms alternatives. More details in §4.1.
  1087→3
  1088→PFF: A Framework for Measuring
  1089→Parametric Faithfulness
  1090→We introduce a framework for measuring the faith-
  1091→fulness of generated reasoning, which we call para-
  1092→metric faithfulness. This framework supports mul-
  1093→tiple ways to measure parametric faithfulness, and
  1094→in §4, we propose one such way.
  1095→Motivation.
  1096→A line of work has analyzed the sen-
  1097→sitivity of models to perturbations applied to rea-

  1110→Word Embeddings Are Steers for Language Models
  1111→Chi Han, Jialiang Xu, Manling Li, Yi Fung, Chenkai Sun,
  1112→Nan Jiang, Tarek Abdelzaher, Heng Ji
  1113→University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign

  1116→Abstract
  1117→Language models (LMs) automatically learn
  1118→word embeddings during pre-training on lan-
  1119→guage corpora. Although word embeddings
  1120→are usually interpreted as feature vectors for
  1121→individual words, their roles in language model
  1122→generation remain underexplored. In this work,
  1123→we theoretically and empirically revisit out-
  1124→put word embeddings and find that their lin-
  1125→ear transformations are equivalent to steering
  1126→language model generation styles. We name
  1127→such steers LM-Steers and find them existing
  1128→in LMs of all sizes. It requires learning param-
  1129→eters equal to 0.2% of the original LMs’ size
  1130→for steering each style. On tasks such as lan-
  1131→guage model detoxification and sentiment con-
  1132→trol, LM-Steers can achieve comparable or su-
  1133→perior performance compared with state-of-the-
  1134→art controlled generation methods while main-
  1135→taining a better balance with generation qual-
  1136→ity. The learned LM-Steer serves as a lens in
  1137→text styles: it reveals that word embeddings are
  1138→interpretable when associated with language
  1139→model generations and can highlight text spans
  1140→that most indicate the style differences. An
  1141→LM-Steer is transferrable between different lan-
  1142→guage models by an explicit-form calculation.
  1143→One can also continuously steer LMs simply
  1144→by scaling the LM-Steer or compose multi-
  1145→ple LM-Steers by adding their transformations.
  1146→Our codes are publicly available at https:
  1147→//github.com/Glaciohound/LM-Steer. 1

  1223→We start by examining the theoretical relation
  1224→between word embeddings and LM generation dis-
  1225→tributions. We find that linear transformations in
  1226→output word embeddings are equivalent to LM gen-
  1227→eration style changes. This motivates our empirical
  1228→investigation on a lightweight and simple steering
  1229→method, LM-Steer, to steer LM generation distribu-
  1230→tion flexibly and transparently. LM-Steer deploys a
  1231→d × d learnable linear transformation W on the out-
  1232→put word embeddings, where d is the embedding
  1233→dimension. Specifically, the embeddings ev are re-
  1234→placed with ev + ϵWev. Here, ϵ acts as a “steering
  1235→value” to provide a simple control on steering po-
  1236→larity and intensity. Inherently, LM-Steer discovers
  1237→hidden dimensions in word embeddings that are
  1238→associated with text styles. By transforming these
  1239→dimensions, LM-Steer affects LMs’ interpretation
  1240→of words and, consequently, generation distribu-
  1241→tions.
  1242→Empirically, we find such LM-Steers exist preva-
  1243→lently in LMs of all sizes, thanks to its general
  1244→formulation and ignorance of model architectures.
  1245→On tasks such as language model detoxification
  1246→and sentiment control, it achieves comparable or
  1247→superior performance compared to the state-of-the-
  1248→art controlled generation baselines. We also find
  1249→multiple merits of LM-Steer both as a steering tool
  1250→and lens for inspecting the relation between word
  1251→embeddings and language model generation. An
  1252→LM-Steer can highlight text spans that best indi-
  1253→cate a style in the full text. A learned LM-Steer also
  1254→makes word embedding dimensions interpretable
  1255→by pointing out the dimensions closest related to a

  1257→contradict a style. LM-Steer is both parameter ef-

  1273→Control of Language Models has been of growing
  1274→interest in recent years, motivated by the increasing
  1275→capabilities of LMs (Li et al., 2023a). This area
  1276→originates from the need to leverage the genera-
  1277→tion capabilities of large language models while
  1278→avoiding the need for time-consuming and costly
  1279→retraining or fine-tuning. Attempts include apply-
  1280→ing attribute classifiers or heuristic constraints at
  1281→decoding time (Kumar et al., 2022; Dathathri et al.;
  1282→Liu et al., 2021; Yang and Klein, 2021), treating
  1283→the generation process as an optimization prob-
  1284→lem over the embedding or token sequences (Ku-
  1285→mar et al., 2021), or post-editing the output (Li
  1286→et al., 2018). These techniques are often compu-
  1287→tationally expensive and rely on suitable external
  1288→classifiers. More recently, prompting-based con-
  1289→trol for large language models has received much
  1290→attention, which, however, relies on the quality
  1291→and availability of large language models (Brown
  1292→et al., 2020; OpenAI, 2023), and may also neces-
  1293→sitate the deliberate training (Raffel et al., 2020;
  1294→Zhou et al., 2023). It can also be challenging to
  1295→design effective prompts for complex or nuanced
  1296→control goals. Parameter-efficient fine-tuning such
  1297→as LoRA (Hu et al., 2021a) focuses on learning low-
  1298→rank approximations of model parameters. How-
  1299→ever, this cannot achieve flexible and transferrable
  1300→language model steering like ours. Probably most
  1301→closely related to our work are attempts to discover
  1302→“steering” vectors or tokens (Subramani et al., 2022;
  1303→Li and Liang, 2021), and also similar work in im-
  1304→age generation (Jahanian et al.; Hu et al., 2021b).
  1305→Different from our model, these efforts focus on
  1306→other applications such as multi-task learning and
  1307→sentence recovery, and the learned vectors are not
  1308→shown to be transferrable or interpretable nor en-
  1309→able flexible control.
  1310→Language Model Detoxification Motivated by the

  1336→Polysemanticity and Capacity in Neural Networks
  1337→Adam Scherlis1, Kshitij Sachan1, Adam S. Jermyn2, Joe Benton, Buck Shlegeris1
  1338→1Redwood Research
  1339→2Flatiron Institute
  1340→Abstract
  1341→Individual neurons in neural networks often represent a mixture of unrelated features. This
  1342→phenomenon, called polysemanticity, can make interpreting neural networks more difficult
  1343→and so we aim to understand its causes. We propose doing so through the lens of feature
  1344→capacity, which is the fractional dimension each feature consumes in the embedding space.
  1345→We show that in a toy model the optimal capacity allocation tends to monosemantically
  1346→represent the most important features, polysemantically represent less important features
  1347→(in proportion to their impact on the loss), and entirely ignore the least important features.
  1348→Polysemanticity is more prevalent when the inputs have higher kurtosis or sparsity and
  1349→more prevalent in some architectures than others. Given an optimal allocation of capacity,
  1350→we go on to study the geometry of the embedding space. We find a block-semi-orthogonal
  1351→structure, with differing block sizes in different models, highlighting the impact of model
  1352→architecture on the interpretability of its neurons.
  1353→1
  1354→Introduction
  1355→Individual neurons in neural networks often represent multiple unrelated features in the input [OMS17,
  1356→OCS+20]. This phenomenon is known as polysemanticity, and makes it more difficult to interpret neural
  1357→networks [OCS+20]. While "feature" is a somewhat fuzzy concept [EHO+22b], there are at least some
  1358→cases where we “know it when we see it”. For example, when the input features are independent random
  1359→variables that do not interact in the data-generating process, neurons that represent combinations of these
  1360→input features can be confidently called polysemantic. In this work we explore how loss functions incentivize
  1361→polysemanticity in this setting, and the structure of the learned solutions.
  1362→Fittingly, there are multiple ways that polysemanticity can manifest. Here we focus on one form that seems
  1363→particularly fundamental, namely superposition [EHO+22b]. Suppose we have a linear layer that embeds
  1364→features which then pass through a layer with a nonlinear activation function. The feature embedding vectors
  1365→might not be orthogonal, in which case multiple neurons (nonlinear units) are involved in representing each
  1366→feature. When there are at least as many features as neurons this means that some neurons represent multiple
  1367→features, and so are polysemantic (Figure 1, right). There are other causes of polysemanticity, e.g. feature
  1368→embedding vectors could be rotated relative to the neuron basis (Figure 1, left), but we do not study these in
  1369→this work.
  1370→Here we build on the work of [EHO+22b], who studied polysemanticity in the context of toy models of
  1371→autoencoders. They found that models can support both monosemantic and polysemantic neurons, that pol-
  1372→ysemantic neurons can perform certain kinds of computations, and that the embedding vectors of features
  1373→o
  1374→--- Page 2 ---
  1375→Rotated Embeddings
  1376→Non-Orthogonal Embeddings
  1377→Figure 1: Feature embedding vectors are shown in two dimensions. The neuron basis corresponds to the
  1378→coordinate axes. Left: rotated embeddings. Right: non-orthogonal embeddings. In both cases the result is
  1379→polysemanticity because each neuron receives some input when either feature is present.
  1380→Figure 2: The marginal loss reduction −∂L/∂Ci is shown for several features as a function of feature capacity
  1381→in our toy model. Circles represent the optimal capacity allocation for a particular total embedding dimension.
  1382→Colors vary to make individual curves more distinguishable.
  1383→feature, and the total capacity across all features is bounded by the dimension of the embedding space.
  1384→Because the model has a limited number of neurons and so a limited number of embedding dimensions,
  1385→there is a trade-off between representing different features. We find that the capacity constraint on individual
  1386→features (0-1) means that many features are either ignored altogether (not embedded) or else allocated a full
  1387→dimension orthogonal to all the other features in the embedding space, depending on the relative importance
  1388→of each feature to the loss. Features are represented polysemantically only when the marginal loss reduction
  1389→of assigning more capacity to each is equal (Figure 2). This neatly explains the sharp “pinning” of features to
  1390→either 0 or 1 capacity noted by [EHO+22b], and gives us a framework for understanding the circumstances
  1391→under which features are represented polysemantically.
  1392→To explore capacity allocation in a concrete model, we instantiate our theory for a one-layer model with
  1393→quadratic activations (Section 3). Our model differs from the Anthropic toy model in that ours uses a different
  1394→activation function to make the math more tractable, and, more importantly, ours is focused on polysemantic
  1395→computation rather than data compression. We contrast these toy models in Figure 3.
  1396→For our toy model we can analytically determine the capacity allocation as a function of feature sparsity
  1397→and importance (i.e. weight in the loss), and so construct a “phase diagram” (Figure 4). While the details
  1398→of our phase diagram differ from those of [EHO+22b], reflecting our different toy model, there are three
  1399→qualitative features that are in good agreement. First, when a feature is much more important than the rest,
  1400→2
  1401→
  1402→--- Page 3 ---
  1403→Figure 3: Comparison between the Anthropic toy model of [EHO+22b] (left) and our toy model (right).
  1404→Model inputs are at the bottom of the diagram and outputs are at the top. The key difference is that the
  1405→Anthropic model studies the compression and recovery of high-dimensional vectors, while ours examines
  1406→how a smaller number of polysemantic neurons can simulate the computation done by a larger number of
  1407→monosemantic ones. Figure kindly provided by Chris Olah.
  1408→it is always represented fully with its own embedding dimension. Second, when a feature is much less
  1409→important than the rest, it is ignored entirely. Finally, in a sparsity-dependent intermediate region features are
  1410→partially represented, sharing embedding dimensions. In addition, this confirms our theoretical expectation
  1411→that capacity is allocated according to how much each feature matters to the loss (a mixture of importance
  1412→and sparsity) and that it is often allocated to fully ignore some features while fully representing others. We
  1413→supplement this with empirical results for a variety of activation functions showing that the phase diagram
  1414→predicts the behavior of a broad family of 2-layer models.
  1415→We then turn to study the geometry of the embedding space (Section 4). When embedding matrices fully
  1416→utilize the available capacity we call them “efficient”. We find that every efficient embedding matrix has a
  1417→block-semi-orthogonal structure, with features partitioned into different blocks. When multiple features in a
  1418→block are present they interfere with each other, causing spurious correlations in the output and hence greater
  1419→loss. Features do not, however, interfere across blocks.
  1420→The blocks in efficient matrices correspond to the polytope structure [EHO+22b] found, with small blocks
  1421→corresponding to features embedded as regular polytopes and large blocks corresponding to less-ordered
  1422→structures. Large- and small-block arrangements come with different advantages. With large blocks there
  1423→is significant freedom to allocate capacity across features, whereas with small blocks there is the additional
  1424→constraint that the capacity of each block be an integer and that the block capacities add up to the total
  1425→capacity. On the other hand, with small blocks the lengths of embedding vectors can be chosen more freely
  1426→because blocks can be scaled independently of each other without affecting the capacity allocation.
  1427→In our quadratic model the embedding matrices in our toy model always have one large block, which is cor-
  1428→respondingly less structured. We expect that differences in architecture can lead to different sizes of blocks,
  1429→which could provide a way to control the extent of polysemanticity in models, alongside other approaches
  1430→such as changing the activation function [EHO+22a].

  1433→2.1
  1434→Definitions
  1435→Suppose we have a model composed of stacks of linear layers with nonlinear activation functions. In each
  1436→layer, the model applies a linear transform to the input vector x to produce

  1439→PAPER: 2406.15518_Steering_Without_Side_Effects__Improving_Post_Deployment_Con.pdf

  1441→--- Page 1 ---
  1442→Steering Without Side Effects: Improving
  1443→Post-Deployment Control of Language Models
  1444→Asa Cooper Stickland1∗
  1445→Alexander Lyzhov1
  1446→Jacob Pfau1
  1447→Salsabila Mahdi1
  1448→Samuel R. Bowman1,2
  1449→1New York University
  1450→2Anthropic, PBC
  1451→Abstract
  1452→Language models (LMs) have been shown to behave unexpectedly post-deployment.
  1453→For example, new jailbreaks continually arise, allowing model misuse, despite
  1454→extensive red-teaming and adversarial training from developers. Given most model
  1455→queries are unproblematic and frequent retraining results in unstable user experi-
  1456→ence, methods for mitigation of worst-case behavior should be targeted. One such
  1457→method is classifying inputs as potentially problematic, then selectively applying
  1458→steering vectors on these problematic inputs, i.e. adding particular vectors to model
  1459→hidden states. However, steering vectors can also negatively affect model perfor-
  1460→mance, which will be an issue on cases where the classifier was incorrect. We
  1461→present KL-then-steer (KTS), a technique that decreases the side effects of steering
  1462→while retaining its benefits, by first training a model to minimize Kullback–Leibler
  1463→(KL) divergence between a steered and unsteered model on benign inputs, then
  1464→steering the model that has undergone this training. Our best method prevents
  1465→44% of jailbreak attacks compared to the original Llama-2-chat-7B model while
  1466→maintaining helpfulness (as measured by MT-Bench) on benign requests almost
  1467→on par with the original LM. To demonstrate the generality and transferability of
  1468→our method beyond jailbreaks, we show that our KTS model can be steered to
  1469→reduce bias towards user-suggested answers on TruthfulQA. Code is available:
  1470→https://github.com/AsaCooperStickland/kl-then-steer.
  1471→1
  1472→Introduction
  1473→Language models have been shown to exhibit unexpected behaviors once deployed, for example,
  1474→threatening users (Roose, 2023) or giving instructions on how to make a bomb (Jiang et al., 2024b;
  1475→Geiping et al., 2024) despite being trained for harmlessness. As models become more capable and
  1476→are deployed in scenarios with increasingly high stakes, avoiding such harmful unexpected behaviors
  1477→is of increasing importance (Anderljung et al., 2023).
  1478→Predicting problematic behaviors ahead of time is difficult. Models may behave differently in new
  1479→contexts or in response to new exploits. This necessitates continuous post-deployment monitoring
  1480→and updates. We may want to adjust how cautious a model is when it has access to a new tool such as
  1481→web search, or when it has access to documents containing sensitive information such as copyrighted
  1482→material. We may also want to encourage a range of other behaviors: increasing the probability of
  1483→using a language in a given locale, decreasing model bias towards user-suggested answers, etc. This
  1484→suggests we want the ability to classify each input as potentially problematic, then add a separate
  1485→intervention for each example in a batch of requests. Ideally, we could quickly update a model

  1525→Figure 1: Schematic overview of our KL-then-steer protocol. The pictured workflow uses harmless-
  1526→ness steering for mitigating jailbreaks, but our method applies generally for improving model safety
  1527→given other threat models. In a second set of experiments, we show that steps 1 and 3 of our protocol
  1528→generalize to mitigating model sycophancy, and that one fine-tuning run (Box 2) generalizes to new
  1529→safety interventions, Section 5.3.
  1530→without having to train and re-deploy it, taking on the associated logistical challenges and risks of
  1531→performance regression.
  1532→Existing work on representation engineering (Turner et al., 2023; Li et al., 2023; Zou et al., 2023a;
  1533→Wang and Shu, 2023; Rimsky et al., 2024; Jorgensen et al., 2023; Lu and Rimsky, 2024; von Rütte
  1534→et al., 2024), also known as activation steering, shows promising results on efficiently controlling
  1535→model behavior post-training. In its simplest form, this just means adding a concept vector to model
  1536→hidden states at inference time. For example, Zou et al. (2023a) increases robustness to jailbreaks by
  1537→using vectors corresponding to refusing harmful requests. Roughly speaking, such steering vectors
  1538→make models slightly more cautious about following requests. They do not represent a method for
  1539→reducing the amount of times the model complies with a dangerous request to near-zero, and we
  1540→do not focus on achieving such high reliability in this paper. We can add a different vector to each
  1541→example in a batch, and the strength of the intervention is adjusted by simply increasing the norm of
  1542→the vector. The memory and compute overhead is minimal since we are dealing with vectors.
  1543→However, we find that applying steering vectors to models using a high enough norm to improve safety,
  1544→or make other behavior modifications, results in performance degradation.2 This is not surprising,
  1545→given that steering vectors are interventions on model hidden states which they were never exposed
  1546→to during training. Previous work measures performance degradation on a simplified version of the
  1547→MMLU multiple-choice QA dataset (Turner et al., 2023), and similarly finds that steering vectors
  1548→decrease performance. We explore a more realistic performance measure for conversational AI,
  1549→namely, the ability of models to produce high-quality open-ended
  1550→--- Page 3 ---
  1551→post-deployment by holding out types of jailbreaks at training time. Our technique mitigates almost
  1552→all capabilities reduction (only a 1.5% reduction in MT-Bench score) while reducing harmful behavior
  1553→(e.g. reducing jailbreak success by 44%) relative to the original model.
  1554→Our main contributions are the following:
  1555→• We introduce a technique for training models to not be as affected by the capabilities
  1556→degradation from applying steering vectors (Section 3.2). As such, we improve adversarial
  1557→robustness using steering vectors without losing general capabilities (Section 5.1).
  1558→• In order to modify the model’s behavior on each individual example out of a batch of
  1559→requests with some criteria, we may need to cheaply classify inputs. We show that simple
  1560→logistic probes on top of hidden states from Llama-2-chat-7b, or a cheap external classifier,
  1561→can classify prompts as likely to elicit bad behavior (Section 5.2).
  1562→• Our KL-then-steer approach generalizes to new tasks, as demonstrated on a benchmark
  1563→testing model preference for user-suggested answers (sycophancy) on TruthfulQA (Lin et al.,
  1564→2022): For a given anti-sycophancy steering vector strength, the KTS model chooses the user-
  1565→suggested answer less often and gets higher accuracy on the correct answer (Section 5.3).
  1566→2
  1567→Background and Related Work
  1568→Interventions on representations and weights of trained language models have shown promise in
  1569→controlling generations and revealing how information gets processed inside models. For example,
  1570→Meng et al. (2022); Hernandez et al. (2023) introduced methods for editing factual knowledge
  1571→encoded inside pretrained models such as GPT-2 (Radford et al., 2019). Subramani et al. (2022)
  1572→pioneered activation steering and showed that it can be used to efficiently control the sentiment of
  1573→GPT-2 generations without fine-tuning. Subsequent works (Turner et al., 2023; Zou et al., 2023a)
  1574→explored simpler versions of steering that don’t require backward passes. In contrast to prior work,
  1575→we focus on the application of steering to realistically control models post-deployment, and as such
  1576→pay closer attention to general degradation in performance. Concurrent to our work, Arditi et al.
  1577→(2024) show that a single direction in activation space mediates refusal behavior in language models.
  1578→Previous approaches to improving model safety typically rely on fine-tuning, for example, reinforce-
  1579→ment learning from human feedback on helpful, harmless, and honest data (Bai et al., 2022). However
  1580→current LLMs increasingly support diverse use-cases and serve as foundations for complex scaffold-
  1581→ing, such as agents designed to carry out software tasks (Yang et al., 2024). As such, responding to
  1582→post-deployment safety threats with whole-model fine-tuning could create unacceptable instability
  1583→by changing model behavior on all users’ workflows. Instead, we propose augmenting monolithic
  1584→fine-tuning of the model to remove new vulnerabilities as they emerge with our approach. Imagine we

  1614→Structure
  1615→Yuxiao Li 4,†,

  1631→Abstract: Sparse autoencoders have recently produced dictionaries of high-dimensional
  1632→vectors corresponding to the universe of concepts represented by large language models.
  1633→We find that this concept universe has interesting structure at three levels: (1) The “atomic”
  1634→small-scale structure contains “crystals” whose faces are parallelograms or trapezoids,
  1635→generalizing well-known examples such as (man:woman::king:queen). We find that the quality
  1636→of such parallelograms and associated function vectors improves greatly when projecting
  1637→out global distractor directions such as word length, which is efficiently performed with
  1638→linear discriminant analysis. (2) The “brain” intermediate-scale structure has significant
  1639→spatial modularity; for example, math and code features form a “lobe” akin to functional
  1640→lobes seen in neural fMRI images. We quantify the spatial locality of these lobes with
  1641→multiple metrics and find that clusters of co-occurring features, at coarse enough scale, also
  1642→cluster together spatially far more than one would expect if feature geometry were random.
  1643→(3) The “galaxy”-scale large-scale structure of the feature point cloud is not isotropic, but
  1644→instead has a power law of eigenvalues with steepest slope in middle layers. We also
  1645→quantify how the clustering entropy depends on the layer.
  1646→Keywords: sparse coding; mechanistic interpretability; neural networks; large language
  1647→models; clustering
  1648→1. Introduction
  1649→While large language models (LLMs) now exhibit a variety of impressive abilities [1–3],
  1650→we largely do not understand the internal cognition that underlies the behavior of these
  1651→systems. This lack of transparency may pos
  1652→--- Page 2 ---
  1653→Entropy 2025, 27, 344
  1654→2 of 16
  1655→earlier studies applying sparse coding to artificial neural network representations [14–16],
  1656→and to earlier work in neuroscience on biological neural representations [17,18]. Underlying
  1657→this work is the idea that neural networks use sparse coding to represent concepts in their
  1658→activation space [19]. In particular, sparse autoencoders are motivated by the assumptions
  1659→that (1) networks compute a variety of “features” from their input, (2) features are repre-
  1660→sented as one-dimensional directions in activation space {di}, (3) features are represented
  1661→simply by adding them to the network’s activations, so activation vectors take the form
  1662→∑i fidi, and (4) the coefficients fi are sparse—only a small subset of all possible features “fire”
  1663→at once. The combination of assumptions (2)–(4) has been called the Linear Representation
  1664→Hypothesis [20–22].
  1665→If these assumptions hold, we could automatically discover these features with sparse
  1666→dictionary learning. Sparse dictionary learning attempts to learn an overcomplete basis
  1667→(dictionary) {di} such that vectors x from a given distribution can be represented as sparse
  1668→linear combinations of dictionary elements. Sparse autoencoders offer a simple approach
  1669→to sparse dictionary learning. Sparse autoencoders consist of a learnable encoder function
  1670→Enc, which maps vectors x ∈Rn to a hidden latent representation f ∈Rm, and a decoder
  1671→Dec, which maps latent f back to ˆx ∈Rn. The objective of the sparse autoencoder is to
  1672→accurately reconstruct the input x from a sparse latent representation, and they are trained
  1673→with gradient descent with a loss function like
  1674→L = ||x −Dec(Enc(x))||2
  1675→2 + λ|| f||0.
  1676→Sparse autoencoders use a linear decoder Dec( f) = Wd f + bd, so that the output of the SAE
  1677→can be interpreted as a linear combination of features: ˆx = ∑i fiWi
  1678→d + bd. In practice, hidden
  1679→latents discovered by sparse autoencoders tend to be more interpretable than neurons,
  1680→activating in more consistent contexts [11,12], suggesting that they may be learning the true
  1681→latents underlying the network’s computation. For AI safety, sparse autoencoders have
  1682→shown some preliminary success: Ref. [7] reports specially training an LLM to have a hidden
  1683→objective, and then challenging separate teams of researchers to identify this objective. One
  1684→team was able to quickly identify this objective by looking at sparse autoencoder features
  1685→that activated when the LLM was prompted to exhibit “potentially concerning behaviors”,
  1686→and then looking at examples in the training data where that same feature fired.
  1687→Although some early work motivating sparse autoencoders suggested that networks
  1688→would arrange features maximally spread apart (approximately orthogonal) [19], recent
  1689→works have suggested that features may have a more sophisticated geometric struc-
  1690→ture [13,22]. Recently, a large collection of SAEs have been made publicly available [23],
  1691→so it is timely to study their structure at various scales. Thus, the present pape
  1692→--- Page 3 ---

  1695→2. Related Work
  1696→Neural network geometry: Many past works have studied the geometry of neural
  1697→network activations. These works find that the intrinsic dimension of neural network
  1698→hidden states are much lower than the full model dimension [24], that nearby vectors in
  1699→activation space are semantically similar [25], and that at local minima well generalizing
  1700→neural network loss landscapes have many “flat” directions [26]. Other works study how
  1701→representations evolve through models; one hypotheses is “iterative inference”, which
  1702→claims that neural networks iteratively refine activations layer by layer [27,28]. A contrast-
  1703→ing hypothesis is a circuits view, which holds that information flows in discrete steps along
  1704→a directed acyclic graph through the model, and representations cleanly change between
  1705→steps [29]. Another work [30] found that representations of hierarchically related concepts
  1706→are orthogonal to each other while categorical concepts are represented as polytopes. Our
  1707→work is in the same vein as these earlier analysis, but differs in an important way because
  1708→we use the SAE basis, which represents the model’s atomic concept space instead of its
  1709→activation space.
  1710→SAE feature structure: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) are a recent approach for discov-
  1711→ering interpretable language model features without supervision, although relatively few
  1712→works have examined SAE feature structure. Bricken et al. [12] and Templeton et al. [13]
  1713→both visualize SAE features with UMAP projections and notice that features tend to group
  1714→together in “neighborhoods” of related features, in contrast to the approximately orthogo-
  1715→nal geometry observed in the toy model of Elhage et al. [19]. Engels et al. [22] find examples
  1716→of SAE structure where multiple SAE features appear to reconstruct a multi-dimensional
  1717→feature with interesting geometry, and multiple authors have recently speculated that SAE
  1718→vectors might contain more important structures [31,32]. Bussmann et al. [33] suggest that
  1719→SAE features are in fact linear combinations of more atomic features, and discover these
  1720→more atomic latents with “meta SAEs”. Our discussion of crystal structure in SAE features
  1721→is related to this idea that seemingly atomic representations might be composed of more
  1722→atomic representations.
  1723→Semantically meaningful linear representations: Early work found that word em-
  1724→bedding methods such as GloVe and Word2vec contained directions encoding semantic
  1725→concepts, e.g., the well-known formula f(king) −f(man) + f(woman) = f(queen) [34–36].
  1726→More recent research has found similar evidence of linear representations in sequence mod-
  1727→els trained only on next token prediction, including Othello board positions [37,38], integer
  1728→lattices [39], the truth value of assertions [40], and numeric quantities such as longitude,
  1729→latitude, birth year, and death year [41,42], inspiring the Linear Representation Hypothesis
  1730→(see above). Recent works have also found causal function vectors for i

  1736→Extracting Latent Steering Vectors from Pretrained Language Models
  1737→Nishant Subramani†
  1738→Nivedita Suresh♦
  1739→Matthew E. Peters†
  1740→†Allen Institute for Artiﬁcial Intelligence, Seattle, WA, USA

  1744→Abstract
  1745→Prior work on controllable text generation has
  1746→focused on learning how to control language
  1747→models through trainable decoding, smart-
  1748→prompt design, or ﬁne-tuning based on a de-
  1749→sired objective. We hypothesize that the infor-
  1750→mation needed to steer the model to generate a
  1751→target sentence is already encoded within the
  1752→model.
  1753→Accordingly, we explore a different
  1754→approach altogether: extracting latent vectors
  1755→directly from pretrained language model de-
  1756→coders without ﬁne-tuning. Experiments show
  1757→that there exist steering vectors, which, when
  1758→added to the hidden states of the language
  1759→model, generate a target sentence nearly per-
  1760→fectly (> 99 BLEU) for English sentences from
  1761→a variety of domains.
  1762→We show that vector
  1763→arithmetic can be used for unsupervised sen-
  1764→timent transfer on the Yelp sentiment bench-
  1765→mark, with performance comparable to mod-
  1766→els tailored to this task. We ﬁnd that distances
  1767→between steering vectors reﬂect sentence sim-
  1768→ilarity when evaluated on a textual similar-
  1769→ity benchmark (STS-B), outperforming pooled
  1770→hidden states of models. Finally, we present an
  1771→analysis of the intrinsic properties of the steer-
  1772→ing vectors. Taken together, our results suggest
  1773→that frozen LMs can be effectively controlled
  1774→through their latent steering space. 1
  1775→1
  1776→Introduction
  1777→Leveraging large pretrained language models
  1778→trained on massive Web corpora has become the
  1779→go-to approach to solve natural language process-
  1780→ing tasks (Peters et al., 2018; Radford et al., 2018;
  1781→Devlin et al., 2018; Brown et al., 2020). As a result,
  1782→controlling these models has become paramount as
  1783→many applications of NLP technology require con-
  1784→trol over the generations of the model. Prior work
  1785→aims to learn how to control language models and
  1786→falls in three categories: trainable decoding (Gu
  1787→1Code is available at https://github.com/nis
  1788→hantsubramani/steering_vectors.
  1789→et al., 2017; Deng et al., 2020), smart-prompt de-
  1790→sign (Shin et al., 2020; Lester et al., 2021), and
  1791→ﬁne-tuning based on a desired objective (Krause
  1792→et al., 2021; Weng, 2021). Further, many works opt
  1793→to train auto-encoder based models for controllable
  1794→text generation (Shen et al., 2017, 2020; Mai et al.,
  1795→2020). These approaches make controllability eas-
  1796→ier by learning a latent space that is more easily
  1797→manipulated to encourage models to generate text
  1798→corresponding to a target attribute such as positive
  1799→sentiment in the case of sentiment transfer.
  1800→We take a more direct approach and explore

1801→whether it is possible to extract latent vectors di-
  1802→rectly from pretrained language model decoders
  1803→without ﬁne-tuning. We call these vectors steering
  1804→vectors and deﬁne the latent steering space of a
  1805→sentence under a language model by the set of ex-
  1806→tracted stee
  1807→--- Page 2 ---
  1808→Figure 1: Our approach adds a vector zsteer to the activations of a pretrained transformer decoder to steer it to
  1809→decode a desired target sentence. We experiment with adding zsteer to different locations inside a GPT-2 model at
  1810→different timesteps. Experiments reveal that our approach can recover sequences nearly perfectly and that injecting
  1811→the steering vector in the middle layers of the transformer stack performs best. Layer normalizations and residual
  1812→connections inside the transformer block are omitted for clarity.
  1813→hidden states and GloVe vectors (Pennington et al.,
  1814→2014) when measuring the cosine similarity be-
  1815→tween vectors, but fall short of lexical methods tai-
  1816→lored to semantic similarity tasks and methods that
  1817→ﬁnetune on natural language inference datasets.
  1818→Lastly, we analyze the intrinsic properties of the
  1819→latent space of our steering vectors. Experiments
  1820→show that decoding from interpolations in the latent
  1821→space produces meaningful output, and that steer-
  1822→ing vectors from different domains cluster together.
  1823→Also, we ﬁnd that our methods do not simply mem-
  1824→orize the target sequence like a naive compression
  1825→algorithm, and instead leverage the model. Taken
  1826→together, our results suggest that frozen language
  1827→models can be controlled effectively through their
  1828→latent steering space.
  1829→2
  1830→Extracting Steering Vectors
  1831→This section discusses our method for extracting a
  1832→steering vector for a target sentence from a frozen,
  1833→pretrained language model. Throughout this pa-
  1834→per, we use GPT2 as our language model and use
  1835→its 117M parameter model size (Radford et al.,
  1836→2019), although our approach can be directly ap-
  1837→plied to any transformer-based autoregressive lan-
  1838→guage model decoder (Vaswani et al., 2017).
  1839→2.1
  1840→Steering Vectors
  1841→In controllable text generation and textual style
  1842→transfer, prior work based on denoising and vari-
  1843→ational autoencoders opt for a disentangling ap-
  1844→proach. These approaches encode the source se-
  1845→quence into a ﬁxed-length vector using an encoder,
  1846→apply style transformations using a controller, and
  1847→ﬁnally decode from the transformed vector using a
  1848→decoder (Shen et al., 2017; Jin et al., 2020). Instead
  1849→of learning an encoder and controller to uncover a
  1850→representation, we ask whether its possible to ex-
  1851→tract a vector directly from a pretrained language
  1852→model decoder in order to steer the model.
  1853→Due to the success of hidden layer manipula-
  1854→tions for language models including adapter-based
  1855→ﬁne-tuning (Houlsby et al., 2019), plug-and-play
  1856→language models (Dathathri et al., 2019), and offset-
  1857→vector-based recovery and style transfer among oth-
  1858→ers (Subramani et al., 2019; Shen et al., 2020; Mai
  1859→et al., 2020; Montero et al., 2021), we choose to
  1860→manipulate the hidden states as well.
  1861→Our method works by adding a ﬁxed-length vec-
  1862→tor zsteer to the hidden states of a pretrained and
  1863→frozen LM. For a desired target sentence, we ran-
  1864→domly initialize zsteer and optimize it via gradient
  1865→descent to maximize the likelihood of the model
  1866→given the ta
  1867→--- Page 3 ---
  1868→feed a zsteer to the model and perform decoding as
  1869→usual. The choice of a ﬁxed-length vector makes
  1870→analysis more meaningful, allowing us to com-
  1871→pare vectors for different sentences with different
  1872→lengths in the same representation space.
  1873→2.2
  1874→Discovering steering vectors
  1875→We deﬁne our steering vectors zsteer ∈Rd′. In
  1876→our experiments, d′ ≤d, where d is the hidden
  1877→dimension of the underlying language model (for
  1878→GPT2-117M, d = 768). If d′ < d, we project
  1879→zsteer using a semi-orthogonal matrix, Wsteer ∈
  1880→Rd′×d, which preserves scale. Wsteer is initialized
  1881→randomly, never trained, and never updated.
  1882→We estimate a steering vector ˆzsteer ∈Rd′ via
  1883→the language model for a sentence x by maximizing
  1884→the log probability of x, while keeping the language
  1885→model ﬁxed:
  1886→ˆzsteer = argmax
  1887→zsteer∈Z

  1895→tic gradient descent with the Adam (Kingma and
  1896→Ba, 2014) optimizer and cross entropy loss to ﬁnd
  1897→the best ˆzsteer, while freezing the language model.
  1898→See algorithm 1 for the pseudocode.
  1899→Since our method adds zsteer to the activations
  1900→of the model, the layer we add zsteer to affects re-
  1901→coverability. We experiment with injecting zsteer at
  1902→different layers (injection locations): at the embed-
  1903→ding layer, right before language model head (LM
  1904→Head), after self-attention layer in the transformer
  1905→stack, after feed-forward layer in the transformer
  1906→stack as well as combinations of them. In addi-
  1907→tion to varying injection locations, we also vary the
  1908→timesteps where zsteer gets added. We experiment
  1909→with adding zsteer at just the ﬁrst timestep and at
  1910→every timestep. See Figure 1 for details.
  1911→2.3
  1912→Steering Language Models
  1913→We steer the language model using zsteer to gener-
  1914→ate a target sentence x by passing in a beginning-
  1915→of-sentence token and zsteer to the model. Since
  1916→we are interested in exact generation, all results
  1917→presented use greedy decoding without assuming
  1918→a true length. We stop when decoding produces
  1919→an end-of-sentence token or produces 1024 tokens,
  1920→the maximum length that GPT-2 can generate.

  1945→Can we extract steering vectors?
  1946→Here, we show that we can robustly extract steering
  1947→vectors that generate target sentences perfectly.
  1948→3.1
  1949→Experimental setup
  1950→We gather a broad corpus spanning four different
  1951→domains and measure the extent to which our ap-
  1952→proach can extract a steering vector for each sen-
  1953→tence under a variety of experimental conditions,
  1954→where we vary injection locations and timesteps.

  1961→IMPROVING STEERING VECTORS BY
  1962→TARGETING SPARSE AUTOENCODER FEATURES
  1963→Sviatoslav Chalnev∗

  1969→ABSTRACT
  1970→To control the behavior of language models, steering methods attempt to ensure that outputs of the
  1971→model satisfy specific pre-defined properties. Adding steering vectors to the model is a promising
  1972→method of model control that is easier than finetuning, and may be more robust than prompt-
  1973→ing.However, it can be difficult to anticipate the effects of steering vectors produced by methods such
  1974→as CAA [Panickssery et al., 2024] or the direct use of SAE latents [Templeton et al., 2024]. In our
  1975→work, we address this issue by using SAEs to measure the effects of steering vectors, giving us a
  1976→method that can be used to understand the causal effect of any steering vector intervention. We use
  1977→this method for measuring causal effects to develop an improved steering method, SAE-Targeted
  1978→Steering (SAE-TS), which finds steering vectors to target specific SAE features while minimizing
  1979→unintended side effects. We show that overall, SAE-TS balances steering effects with coherence
  1980→better than CAA and SAE feature steering, when evaluated on a range of tasks.
  1981→1
  1982→Introduction
  1983→There are widespread calls for better control of the behaviour of Large Language Models (LLMs; e.g. The White House
  1984→[2023]). Current methods such as prompting [Wallace et al., 2024] and finetuning [Ouyang et al., 2022, Chung et al.,
  1985→2022] offer some degree of control, but have clear limitations. For example, prompting can be fragile and is often
  1986→susceptible to methods that can subvert these instructions [Wei et al., 2023]. Finetuning a model can be more robust but
  1987→requires a curated dataset for training which can be both expensive and time-consuming to produce (e.g. Dubey et al.
  1988→[2024]).
  1989→Steering vectors [Turner et al., 2024] have the potential to be more robust than prompting, and both cheaper and easier
  1990→to implement than finetuning. Steering vectors work by adding activations to the hidden state of a model, part way
  1991→through the forward pass (Section 3). By generating and inserting activation vectors into the model’s forward pass, we
  1992→can steer the model towards desired behaviors.
  1993→However, a problem with current steering methods is their unpredictability – it’s often unclear exactly how a steering
  1994→vector will affect model behavior. Steering vectors may not produce the intended changes in the model’s output or may
  1995→cause unforeseen behaviors, as we discuss in Section 3. In some cases, steering interventions produce no interpretable
  1996→changes in model behavior other than model degradation, as we show in Section 5. This unpredictability makes it
  1997→difficult to precisely control the model.
  1998→To address these challenges, we develop a method for quantifying the effects of a steering intervention on model outputs.
  1999→We use Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs; Ng [2011], Cunningham et al. [2023], Bricken et al. [2023]) to measure
